1: When you sit down to eat with a ruler, give careful thought to what is before you — the meal as a place of discernment and formation. Table fellowship carries enormous weight in the NT: Jesus ate with sinners (Luke 15:1-2), shared the Last Supper with his disciples (Luke 22:14-20), and appeared at meals after the resurrection (Luke 24:30-35; John 21:12-13). The call to 'give careful thought to what is before you' has eucharistic resonance in Paul's warning: 'Let a person examine himself, then, and so eat of the bread and drink of the cup' (1 Corinthians 11:28). Every meal is a moment of discernment.
2: Put a knife to your throat if you have an unruly appetite — the radical language of self-denial in the face of dangerous desire. Jesus uses the same rhetoric of radical self-denial: 'If your right eye causes you to sin, tear it out and throw it away' (Matthew 5:29); 'If anyone would come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross' (Matthew 16:24). The 'knife to the throat' is not literal self-harm but the drastic language of wisdom that takes the power of appetite seriously and requires radical counter-measures.
3: Do not crave his delicacies, for that food is deceptive — the appearance of benefit masking the reality of manipulation. Discernment about what appears beneficial but is actually deceptive runs through the NT: 'Even Satan disguises himself as an angel of light' (2 Corinthians 11:14); the false prophet who appears as a lamb but speaks like a dragon (Revelation 13:11). The deceiving delicacy is the pattern of the temptation in Eden (Genesis 3:6: good for food, a delight to the eyes) repeated in every enticing but corrupting offer.
4: Do not wear yourself out trying to get rich; have the wisdom to show restraint — the limit on wealth-acquisition as wisdom's discipline. Jesus says 'Do not lay up for yourselves treasures on earth, where moth and rust destroy and where thieves break in and steal, but lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven' (Matthew 6:19-20). The wisdom to show restraint in the pursuit of wealth is the posture of those who have found the treasure of the kingdom (Matthew 13:44) and are content with what they have (Hebrews 13:5).
5: Will you exhaust your eyes chasing what is not there? Riches sprout wings and fly away like an eagle — the vanishing nature of wealth. The parable of the rich fool (Luke 12:16-21) dramatizes this exactly: 'Soul, you have ample goods laid up for many years; relax, eat, drink, be merry. But God said to him, Fool! This night your soul is required of you.' The eagling away of wealth is the wisdom perspective on what death makes visible: only the investment that outlasts death has lasting value (Matthew 6:20).
6: Do not eat the bread of a stingy man; do not covet his choice food — the discernment about false hospitality. The NT extends this: be wary of those who come offering apparent friendship but harbor contempt. Christ is the model of true generosity: 'I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger' (John 6:35). The bread of the stingy man leaves you hungry; the bread of Christ satisfies permanently. The call to discern bread sources is the wisdom preparation for recognizing the one who offers true nourishment.
7: He keeps a running count in his mind; he says 'Eat and drink,' but his heart is not with you — the exposure of false welcome. Jesus confronts exactly this in the Pharisees: outward hospitality concealing inward calculation. 'Beware of the leaven of the Pharisees, which is hypocrisy' (Luke 12:1). The contrast is Christ himself whose 'heart is with you' in the fullest sense: 'As the Father has loved me, so have I loved you' (John 15:9) — a welcome with nothing hidden, nothing calculated, nothing withheld.
8: You will vomit up the little you ate, and your pleasant words will have been wasted — the futility of participating in false fellowship. The wasted investment in false relationships and manipulative company is the wisdom warning about misplaced trust. The NT's alternative: 'Build one another up, just as you are doing' (1 Thessalonians 5:11); fellowship grounded in Christ is the antidote to the empty returns of manipulative company. True community is formed around the one whose welcome is unconditional.
9: Do not speak in the hearing of a fool, for he will despise the wisdom of your words — the wisdom of audience discernment. Jesus gives the same guidance: 'Do not throw your pearls before pigs, lest they trample them underfoot and turn to attack you' (Matthew 7:6). The selectivity about who receives wisdom is not elitism but realism — wisdom must be offered where it can take root. The parable of the soils (Matthew 13:1-23) shows Christ himself practicing this discernment: the same seed is scattered widely, but only some ground receives it.
10: Do not move an ancient boundary stone; do not enter the fields of orphans — the covenant protection of the most vulnerable. The boundary-stone ethics of covenant community are combined here with the orphan-protection mandate (Deuteronomy 10:18: God executes justice for the fatherless). Jesus intensifies the stakes: whoever causes one of these little ones to stumble, it would be better for him to have a millstone hung around his neck (Matthew 18:6). The protection of the vulnerable is not a secondary concern but a central marker of kingdom ethics.
11: For their Redeemer is strong; he will plead their case against you — the gō'ēl (kinsman-redeemer) of the fatherless. This is among the most directly Christological verses in Proverbs. Christ is identified as Redeemer (Heb: gō'ēl) throughout the NT: 'We have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous' (1 John 2:1). He is the strong Redeemer who pleads the case of those who cannot plead for themselves, 'always living to make intercession for them' (Hebrews 7:25). The anonymous Redeemer of Proverbs 23:11 is the pre-incarnate shadow of the Advocate who bears the name of Jesus.
12: Devote your heart to instruction and your ears to words of knowledge — the dual commitment of wisdom reception. The NT's 'faith comes from hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ' (Romans 10:17) identifies the ear as the instrument of saving reception. 'Devoting the heart' is the posture of the disciple who does not merely hear but integrates: 'Be doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving yourselves' (James 1:22). Christ both models total receptivity to the Father's word and is himself the word to which the heart must be devoted.
13: Do not withhold discipline from a child; if you strike him with the rod, he will not die — the necessity of formative discipline. Hebrews 12:5-6 quotes Proverbs 3:11-12 and extends the principle: 'Do not regard lightly the discipline of the Lord... for the Lord disciplines the one he loves.' Proverbs 23:13-14 is the parent's side of the same truth: withholding discipline is a failure of love. The formative rod that Proverbs commends is a shadow of God's fatherly discipline that the NT calls believers to receive without despising.
14: If you discipline him with the rod, you will save his life from death — discipline as an act of salvation. The connection between discipline and rescue from death is the wisdom principle. Christ's own formation — 'He learned obedience through what he suffered' (Hebrews 5:8) — is the supreme instance: the discipline of the cross saves many from death. The rod that saves the child from death is a shadow of the suffering that saves humanity; discipline is always, at its core, a redemptive act.
15: My son, if your heart is wise, then my heart will be glad as well — the joy of the wisdom-teacher in the student's formation. Jesus applies this to his own relationship with his disciples: 'These things I have spoken to you, that my joy may be in you, and that your joy may be full' (John 15:11). The Father's joy in the Son who learned wisdom (Luke 2:52) and the wisdom-teacher's joy in the growing student are the same pattern: God delights in the formation of his children, and Christ is the first and fullest recipient of that delight.
16: Yes, my inmost being will rejoice when your lips speak what is right — the wisdom-teacher's delight in the student's right speech. The connection between inner formation and right speech runs through the NT: 'Out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaks' (Matthew 12:34). What the lips speak is the measure of the heart's formation. Christ himself is the one in whose mouth 'no guile was found' (1 Peter 2:22; Isaiah 53:9) — the model of right speech that flows from a perfectly formed inner life.
17: Do not let your heart envy sinners, but live in the fear of the LORD all day long — the sustained posture of covenant trust over against envy. Paul reframes this: 'I have learned, in whatever situation I am, to be content' (Philippians 4:11). The envy of the wicked who seem to prosper is addressed throughout Psalm 73, and its resolution is the same as Proverbs 23:17: the perspective of the sanctuary, where the end of the wicked is seen clearly. Contentment in the fear of the LORD is the posture of those who have set their hope on Christ rather than on the apparent success of the ungodly.
18: For surely there is a future, and your hope will not be cut off — the eschatological confidence that sustains the wisdom-walker. The NT intensifies and personalizes this hope: 'We rejoice in hope of the glory of God' (Romans 5:2); 'blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ! According to his great mercy, he has caused us to be born again to a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead' (1 Peter 1:3). The 'future' that Proverbs promises is the resurrection — guaranteed by Christ's own rising, the firstfruits (1 Corinthians 15:20).
19: Listen, my son, and be wise; keep your heart on the right path — the simplest possible statement of the wisdom call. The heart that keeps to the right path is the single-minded heart of Matthew 6:22-23: 'The eye is the lamp of the body. So, if your eye is healthy, your whole body will be full of light.' Christ calls his disciples to the undivided heart that wisdom describes — the 'pure in heart' who will see God (Matthew 5:8). Formation begins with listening; it produces a heart aligned with the way.
20: Do not be among those who drink too much wine or those who gorge themselves on meat — the warning against excess. The NT's positive form: 'Do not get drunk with wine... but be filled with the Spirit' (Ephesians 5:18). The drunkenness that Proverbs warns against is a counterfeit of the Spirit's fullness — both involve a kind of overwhelming, but one leads to waste and the other to worship. Christ was falsely accused of being a 'glutton and a drunkard' (Matthew 11:19) for eating with sinners; the accusation reveals how completely he rejected the abstinence of the desert while also not practicing excess.
21: For the drunkard and the glutton will come to poverty, and drowsiness will clothe them with rags — the progressive impoverishment of excess. The NT's 'the desires of the flesh and the desires of the eyes and pride of life are not from the Father but are from the world. And the world is passing away along with its desires' (1 John 2:16-17) identifies the trajectory: desires that are not disciplined lead to the poverty and rags that Proverbs predicts. Christ offers the opposite: 'I counsel you to buy from me gold refined by fire, so that you may be rich... and white garments so that you may clothe yourself' (Revelation 3:18).
22: Listen to your father who gave you life; do not despise your mother when she is old — the fifth commandment applied to the aging parent. Jesus reinforced this command against the Corban tradition (Mark 7:9-13: setting aside parental support in the name of religious obligation). He himself honored his mother even from the cross (John 19:26-27), commending her to the beloved disciple. The honoring of parents is not suspended by the kingdom but deepened: those who do the will of the Father are Christ's family (Matthew 12:50), not a replacement for biological family but an extension of the covenant of honor.
23: Buy the truth and do not sell it — wisdom, instruction, and understanding as well — truth as the incomparably precious commodity. Jesus declares himself 'the truth' (John 14:6): to buy truth is, ultimately, to find Christ. Paul describes his own biographical enactment: 'I count everything as loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord... I have suffered the loss of all things and count them as rubbish, in order that I may gain Christ' (Philippians 3:8). The instruction not to sell truth is the call never to trade away one's hold on Christ for any lesser gain.
24: The father of a righteous child will greatly rejoice; the one who fathers a wise son will be glad — the joy of godly parenthood. The father who runs to meet the prodigal son (Luke 15:20) is the portrait of divine joy at human return to righteousness. More profoundly, the Father's declaration over Jesus — 'This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased' (Matthew 3:17) — is the climax of this verse's hope: a Father rejoicing in the perfect righteousness and wisdom of his Son. All fathers who rejoice in righteous children share, in miniature, the Father's delight in the Son.
25: Let your father and mother be glad; let the one who bore you rejoice — the ripple of the child's righteousness blessing the parents. Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:46-55) is in part the expression of a mother's joy in a righteous Son. The rejoicing of Elizabeth (Luke 1:41-45) and Simeon (Luke 2:28-32) and Anna (Luke 2:38) at the birth of Jesus are the community's participation in the joy that a righteous child brings. Every instance of a child's wisdom bringing joy to parents is a participation in the pattern established at the birth of the one who is wisdom.
26: My son, give me your heart, and let your eyes keep watch over my ways — the central demand of wisdom: total commitment of the heart. The NT's form: 'You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind and with all your strength' (Mark 12:30). Jesus makes the giving of the heart the first and greatest commandment. Proverbs 23:26 is the wisdom form of what Jesus declares to be the summary of all divine demand: give me your whole heart. Wisdom calls for what only the gospel can produce — a heart genuinely given.
27: For a prostitute is a deep pit, and an immoral woman is a narrow well — the danger of covenant unfaithfulness. The pit and well imagery of spiritual death is inverted in John 4: at a well, Jesus offers 'living water' to the woman who has known covenant failure (John 4:10-14). The narrow well of Proverbs 23:27 becomes the location where Christ offers what no pit can give — water welling up to eternal life. The spiritual danger that the adulteress represents is the exact problem Christ addresses at the well: the thirst that false love cannot satisfy.
28: She lies in wait like a hunter and multiplies the unfaithful among men — the corrupting power of covenant-breaking desire. The NT: 'Each person is tempted when he is lured and enticed by his own desire. Then desire when it has conceived gives birth to sin, and sin when it is fully grown brings forth death' (James 1:14-15). The spreading of unfaithfulness is the pattern of every corrupting influence. Christ's counter-work is the converse: his faithfulness draws others into faithfulness, multiplying the covenant-keepers as the adulteress multiplies the faithless.
29: Who has grief? Who has anguish? Who has quarreling? Who has needless wounds? Who has bloodshot eyes? — the diagnostic questions about the fruit of drunkenness. The answer follows: those who linger over wine. The NT's diagnostic is broader: 'What causes quarrels and what causes fights among you? Is it not this, that your passions are at war within you?' (James 4:1). The grief and anguish that uncontrolled appetite produces are the outward signs of the inner warfare from which Christ promises liberation: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest' (Matthew 11:28).
30: Those who linger over wine and go looking for spiced wine — the portrait of addictive pursuit. The NT addresses the same dynamic with a structural counter: 'Do not get drunk with wine, for that is debauchery, but be filled with the Spirit' (Ephesians 5:18). The seeking of spiced wine is redirected toward the seeking of the Spirit's filling. Christ transforms the water into wine at Cana (John 2:1-11), signaling that the era of abundance has arrived — but an abundance of the Spirit, not the wine that seduces.
31: Do not look at wine when it is red and sparkling in the cup — the seduction of the eye as the gateway to the will. The eye as the entry point for temptation is a consistent wisdom theme (Job 31:1: I have made a covenant with my eyes). Jesus: 'The eye is the lamp of the body. If your eye is healthy, your whole body will be full of light' (Matthew 6:22). The sparkling wine that catches the eye represents every attractive thing that wisdom calls us to look away from, directing the gaze instead to what is truly worth seeing.
32: In the end it bites like a snake and stings like a viper — the deferred consequence of wine's seduction. The bite that comes after the pleasure mirrors the pattern of sin: 'The wages of sin is death' (Romans 6:23). Paul uses the snake metaphor for the same: 'the serpent deceived Eve by his cunning' (2 Corinthians 11:3). The viper-bite of Proverbs 23:32 connects to the serpent of Eden whose bite entered history and whose undoing comes through the seed of the woman (Genesis 3:15) — Christ, who was struck but struck back, dying and rising.
33: Your eyes will see strange things; your heart will speak perverse things — the mental and moral distortion of drunkenness. The perverse heart that speaks perversion under the influence of wine is the picture of disordered desire. 'Out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaks' (Matthew 12:34): the perverse heart produces perverse speech. Christ's transformation reverses this: 'I will give you a new heart, and a new spirit I will put within you... I will put my Spirit within you' (Ezekiel 36:26-27). The new heart that speaks what is right is the gift of Christ through the Spirit.
34: You will be like one asleep in the middle of the sea, like one sprawled on top of the rigging — the vulnerability of the person overwhelmed by wine. Jonah slept in the ship's hold during the storm (Jonah 1:5) while the sailors cried out to their gods; his sleep represented his flight from God. The ironic inversion: Jesus slept in the boat during the storm (Mark 4:38) not from the stupor of flight but from the peace of one who commands the sea. The drunken sailor asleep on the rigging contrasts with the sovereign Christ asleep in the stern.
35: 'They beat me, but I felt no pain; they struck me, but I did not know it. When will I wake up so I can find another drink?' — the pathological insensibility and compulsion of addiction. The one who cannot feel the blows mirrors the hardened heart: 'Having become callous they have given themselves up to sensuality' (Ephesians 4:19). The compulsive 'when will I wake up' is the counter-image of the resurrection's waking: 'Awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you' (Ephesians 5:14). The insensible drinker and the risen disciple represent the two ends of the spectrum — spiritual death and the waking of new life in Christ.
